title: Haemolytic anaemia
body_html:
Definition
Haemolytic anaemia refers to a reduction in red blood cell (RBC) lifespan due to premature destruction (haemolysis), leading to anaemia when bone marrow compensation is inadequate. It can be intravascular (within blood vessels) or extravascular (primarily in the spleen or liver).

Classification

TypeSubtypes / Examples
Hereditary- Membrane defects: Hereditary spherocytosis, elliptocytosis
- Enzyme defects: G6PD deficiency, Pyruvate kinase deficiency
- Haemoglobinopathies: Sickle cell disease, Thalassaemia
Acquired- Autoimmune (warm/cold antibody types – Coombs-positive)
- Alloimmune: Haemolytic transfusion reaction, Haemolytic disease of the newborn
- Microangiopathic: TTP, HUS, DIC
- Infectious: Malaria
- Chemical/drug-induced
- Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH)
- Secondary to liver or renal disease

Pathophysiology
Normal RBC lifespan ≈ 120 days; in haemolytic anaemia, destruction may occur in days. Erythropoietic hyperplasia and bone marrow expansion may compensate significantly before anaemia becomes evident.

MechanismFeatures
ExtravascularRBCs phagocytosed by macrophages in spleen/liver. Haem → protoporphyrin → bilirubin → bile.
IntravascularRBCs lyse in circulation. Haemoglobin released into plasma → filtered by kidneys → haemoglobinuria. Toxic to renal tubules.

Clinical features
  • General signs of anaemia: fatigue, pallor, dyspnoea
  • Jaundice (unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia)
  • Splenomegaly
  • Pigment gallstones
  • Dark urine (if intravascular)
  • Frontal bossing / skeletal deformities (e.g. β-thalassaemia major)
  • Leg ulcers (e.g. sickle cell anaemia)
  • Parvovirus-induced aplastic crisis
  • Megaloblastic anaemia due to folate depletion

Investigations

TestFinding
FBCLow or normal Hb; ↑ MCV if reticulocytosis is high
Reticulocyte count↑ (unless marrow suppressed)
Peripheral smearPolychromasia, anisopoikilocytosis, spherocytes, schistocytes
Unconjugated bilirubin↑
LDH↑ (due to RBC breakdown)
Haptoglobin↓ (binds free haemoglobin; depleted in intravascular haemolysis)
Urine↑ Urobilinogen (extravascular); Haemoglobinuria, haemosiderinuria (intravascular)
Direct Coombs TestPositive in autoimmune haemolytic anaemia
Bone marrow biopsyErythroid hyperplasia

Important notes
  • Marrow compensation can delay symptoms despite severe haemolysis.
  • Intravascular haemolysis causes: transfusion reactions, G6PD deficiency, TTP/HUS/DIC, some autoimmune HA, PNH.
  • Complications may include renal failure, gallstones, and folate deficiency.
